diagrams.title: Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)
diagrams.description: Shows how multiple signals at different frequencies are combined into a single channel by allocating non-overlapping frequency bands to each signal. The diagram displays the frequency spectrum from low to high, with Signal A occupying the lower band, Signal B in the middle, and Signal C in the higher band, all passing through a multiplexer onto one transmission medium.
elements: Three input signals with different carrier frequencies
Combined composite signal on single channel
Frequency band labels for each signal
diagrams.purpose: Explains how FDM divides the available bandwidth into distinct frequency channels, allowing multiple signals to transmit simultaneously without interfering. Essential for understanding analog signal multiplexing in radio and television broadcasting.

diagrams.title: Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)
diagrams.description: Illustrates how multiple digital signals share the same frequency channel by dividing it into discrete time slots. The diagram shows three input signals (A, B, C) each sampled at regular intervals, with their samples interleaved into a single time-multiplexed stream. Time slots are clearly marked and repeated in a cyclic pattern.
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates how TDM allocates fixed time slots to each signal, enabling digital data from multiple sources to share one communication channel. Critical for understanding digital telephone systems and packet switching fundamentals.

diagrams.title: Wavelength Division Multiplexing (WDM)
diagrams.description: Depicts how multiple optical signals at different wavelengths (colors) are combined onto a single fiber optic cable. Similar to FDM but operating in the optical domain. The diagram shows input light beams of different wavelengths (λ1, λ2, λ3) being combined by an optical multiplexer, transmitted through fiber, and separated by an optical demultiplexer at the destination.
elements: Multiple laser light sources (different colors representing wavelengths)
Light beam visualization with color coding
diagrams.purpose: Explains WDM as the optical equivalent of FDM, used in high-capacity fiber optic networks to multiply bandwidth by combining multiple light wavelengths. Key concept for understanding modern telecommunications backbone networks and dense WDM (DWDM) technology.
